---
title: "Block Capstone Project"
subtitle: "SAT Solver \\& Self-Reference Engine"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    geometry:
      - margin=0.85in
    fontsize: 11pt
    colorlinks: true
    number-sections: false
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{booktabs}
      \usepackage{amssymb}
      \usepackage{amsmath}
      \usepackage{tikz}
      \usepackage{enumitem}
      \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning}
      \setlist{nosep, leftmargin=1.5em}
      \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.35}
---

\begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Time estimate} & \~{}3.5 hours \\
\textbf{Languages} & C++ (SAT solver) + Python (G\"odel numbering \& trust checker) \\
\textbf{Output} & Working SAT solver + self-referential demos + essay \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\vspace{1em}

Build a SAT solver from scratch, then explore self-reference and its implications for AI alignment.

# Part 1 --- DPLL SAT Solver (C++, \~1.5h)

**Goal:** Implement the Davis--Putnam--Logemann--Loveland algorithm.

## 1a. CNF representation

A formula in conjunctive normal form (CNF) is a conjunction of clauses, where each clause is a disjunction of literals:
$$(x_1 \lor \lnot x_3) \land (x_2 \lor x_3) \land (\lnot x_1 \lor \lnot x_2)$$

Represent as a vector of vectors of signed integers: positive = variable, negative = negated.

## 1b. DPLL algorithm

```
DPLL(formula, assignment):
    formula = unit_propagate(formula, assignment)
    formula = pure_literal_eliminate(formula, assignment)

    if formula is empty: return SAT
    if any clause is empty: return UNSAT

    pick an unassigned variable x
    if DPLL(formula with x=True, ...) == SAT: return SAT
    if DPLL(formula with x=False, ...) == SAT: return SAT
    return UNSAT
```

- **Unit propagation:** if a clause has one literal, that literal must be true. Assign it and simplify.
- **Pure literal elimination:** if a variable appears only positive (or only negative) across all clauses, assign it to satisfy those clauses.

## 1c. Test problems

| Problem | Encoding | Expected |
|:--------|:---------|:---------|
| Simple 3-variable | $(x_1 \lor x_2) \land (\lnot x_1 \lor x_3) \land (\lnot x_2 \lor \lnot x_3)$ | SAT |
| Contradiction | $(x_1) \land (\lnot x_1)$ | UNSAT |
| Graph 3-coloring | Encode ``can the Petersen graph be 3-colored?'' | SAT |
| Sudoku | Encode a 4$\times$4 Sudoku as SAT | SAT (print solution) |

For the Sudoku encoding: each cell $(r,c)$ with digit $d$ gets a variable $x_{r,c,d}$. Clauses enforce: each cell has at least one digit, at most one digit, rows/columns/boxes have distinct digits.

# Part 2 --- G\"odel Numbering \& Self-Reference (Python, \~1h)

**Goal:** Encode logical formulas as numbers and construct self-referential sentences.

## 2a. G\"odel numbering scheme

Assign a code to each symbol:

| Symbol | Code |
|:-------|:-----|
| $\lnot$ | 1 |
| $\land$ | 2 |
| $\lor$ | 3 |
| $\Rightarrow$ | 4 |
| ( | 5 |
| ) | 6 |
| $x_i$ | $10 + i$ |

Encode a formula as the product of primes raised to the symbol codes:
$$\ulcorner \lnot x_1 \urcorner = 2^1 \cdot 3^{11} = 2 \cdot 177147 = 354294$$

Implement `encode(formula_string)` and `decode(number)`. Verify roundtrip for 5 formulas.

## 2b. The diagonal lemma (demonstration)

The diagonal lemma says: for any property $P$, there exists a sentence $G$ such that $G \leftrightarrow P(\ulcorner G \urcorner)$.

Demonstrate this concretely:

- Define a simple ``provability'' predicate (e.g., ``is the G\"odel number even?'')
- Use self-application: construct a program that computes its own G\"odel number and checks the predicate on it
- Print: ``This sentence's G\"odel number is [N]. The predicate evaluates to [True/False].''

# Part 3 --- Trust Checker \& Impossibility (Python, \~1h)

## 3a. Toy trust checker

Build a simple proof verifier for propositional logic:

- Represent proofs as sequences of formulas, each justified by an axiom or inference rule (modus ponens)
- The checker accepts a proof if every step is valid

## 3b. L\"ob's theorem demonstration

L\"ob's theorem: if a system can prove ``if I can prove $P$, then $P$'', then the system can already prove $P$.

Demonstrate the implication: write a trust checker that tries to verify its own soundness (``every formula I prove is true''). Show that this self-verification either:

- Fails (the checker can't prove its own soundness --- G\"odel's second incompleteness theorem)
- Or succeeds vacuously (it proves everything, making it unsound)

Print a clear explanation of which case occurs and why.

## 3c. Short essay (in comments, \~200 words)

Compare these four impossibility results and explain how each constrains AI alignment:

1. **Halting problem** --- can't decide if arbitrary programs terminate
2. **Rice's theorem** --- can't decide any nontrivial property of programs
3. **G\"odel's incompleteness** --- sufficiently powerful systems can't prove their own consistency
4. **L\"ob's theorem** --- self-improving AI can't prove its successor maintains alignment

# Deliverables

\begin{enumerate}
  \item C++ SAT solver passes all 4 test problems (including Sudoku solution)
  \item G\"odel encoding/decoding roundtrip works for 5 formulas
  \item Trust checker demonstrates the impossibility of self-verification
  \item Essay on impossibility results and alignment (in code comments)
\end{enumerate}
